narrative:
Torchlake Engineering Group is a six-person consulting engineering firm
serving building owners and plant operators around the Great Lakes. The
work is field-first: facility condition assessments walked floor by floor,
and commissioning of controls retrofits where the firm's engineers stand in
the mechanical room until the sequence of operations does what the drawings
say. Documents are written to be read on a job site: engagement letters
that fit on two pages, kickoff memos with checklists, meeting minutes that
name every attendee and every deficiency owner, and status reports a
facilities director can forward to a CFO without a phone call. The
President signs engagement letters; engineers sign their own field notes.
Tone is direct, technical where it counts, never salesy. People go by first
names in the field and full names with titles on anything a client files.
instructions:
Author every document briefed in `docs`, as JSON matching the deliverable
schema:

  {"schema_id": "orgsmith/authoring-deliverable@1",
   "work_order_id": "wo:author:0003",
   "docs": [{"schema_id": "orgsmith/docir@1", "doc_id": "d:....",
             "blocks": [...]}, ...]}

Block kinds: heading (text, level), paragraph (text), list (items),
table (header, rows), sigblock (signers = person ids from the brief).

Hard rules:
- One DocIR per brief; doc_ids must match the work order exactly.
- Every fact id briefed for a doc MUST appear as a literal placeholder
  {{fact:<id>}} at least once in that doc's text. You do not know fact
  values; never write a number, date, or name in place of a placeholder.
- Use only placeholders for fact ids briefed on that document.
- Do not invent people, organizations, addresses, amounts, or dates.
  People and their titles come from the briefs; the org narrative sets
  tone only.
- Every entry in a doc's `mentions` list must appear VERBATIM (the exact
  `surface` string) somewhere in that doc's text. Sigblock signers count
  as mentions of themselves. Short surfaces are nicknames: work them into
  prose naturally ("...as {{nickname}} noted..."), alongside, not instead
  of, the person's full name.
- Write plain, era-appropriate business prose in the org's voice.

docs.title: Engagement Letter: Controls Retrofit Commissioning for Vazquez, Sanchez and Gutierrez

authors.name: David Jones
authors.title: President
authors.persona: David Jones founded Torchlake after twenty years running plant engineering for an industrial operator, and he still writes like a plant guy: findings first, causes second, niceties never. He signs every engagement letter, reads every field report before it leaves, and strikes any sentence a facilities director could not act on.

participants.name: Jeff Fowler
participants.title: Engineering Manager
participants.persona: Jeff Fowler runs delivery as Engineering Manager and owns the kickoff memo and status report cadence. He thinks in checklists and owner-by-owner tables, keeps the schedule honest, and always names which Fowler he means since his brother-in-arms confusion with David Fowler is a running office joke. Businesslike on the page, dry in person.

participants.name: John Johnson
participants.title: Project Engineer
participants.persona: John Johnson, Project Engineer, is the firm's default minute-taker and deficiency-list keeper. He records meetings nearly verbatim, tags every action with an owner and a due date, and follows up without being reminded. His prose is flat by design; he saves opinions for the drive back from site.

docs.engagement_summary: Controls Retrofit Commissioning for Vazquez, Sanchez and Gutierrez, running about 6 months. Commercial terms and dates are briefed as fact placeholders only.

facts.hint: amount of money; place where the figure belongs
calendar date; place where the date belongs
proper name; place where the name belongs

docs.guidance: Formal letter on firm letterhead confirming the engagement: scope, approach, team, fee and start date, closing. End with a sigblock block signed by the letter's author and the client contact. 3-6 paragraphs.
